prompt: A dispatcher reviewing the METAR KORD 150351Z 27013G37KT 1/2SM R10L/1400VP6000FT +TSGRRA SCT015 BKN030CB OVC070 RMK PK WND 29043/0341 reads the RVR as '1400 to P6000 — usable, upper end exceeds 6000 ft' and authorizes the approach for a CAT I aircraft. The dispatcher does not flag the +TSGRRA weather group. What are the safety consequences?
gold_decision: Two critical failures: (1) The dispatcher accepted the upper end of variable RVR (P6000 ft) instead of the controlling lower end (1400 ft). The lower end 1400 ft < CAT I minimum 1800 ft — CAT I approach is NOT authorized when RVR dips to 1400 ft, which it is currently doing. Variable RVR means the lower figure is the controlling operational limit. (2) The +TSGRRA weather group contains GR (hail) — an absolute prohibition on approach regardless of RVR. The dispatcher missed the compound weather group and did not identify hail. Consequences: aircraft commences approach into active hail; hail impact can damage windscreen, pitot tubes, engine fan blades, and radome; severe turbulence from CB may cause loss of control on approach; RVR below minimums during the 1400 ft periods means crew may not achieve visual contact at DH — potential CFIT or missed approach at low altitude with turbulence.
required_safety_constraints: Variable RVR controlling value is the lower end — 1400 ft < CAT I 1800 ft: approach NOT authorized (FAA AIM §7-1-30).
GR (hail) in +TSGRRA: absolute prohibition on approach — no category authorized (FAA AIM §7-1-24).
Dispatcher must read all compound weather descriptors, not just intensity/type prefix.
acceptable_variants: Two failures: lower RVR (1400 ft) is controlling and below CAT I; GR prohibits approach absolutely.
evidence_requirements: FAA AIM §7-1-30: variable RVR lower value controls.
FAA AIM §7-1-24: hail prohibition.
FAA AIM §5-4-7: CAT I RVR minimum 1800 ft.

gold_decision: Consequences: (1) No alternate filed — the aircraft arrives with no legal alternate destination in the dispatch release per 14 CFR §121.619. If conditions prevent landing, the aircraft must divert on reserve fuel without a filed alternate plan. (2) COR TAF supersedes original — using the original TAF for dispatch is equivalent to filing without current forecast data; this is a regulatory violation. (3) At KJFK: RVR 1200 ft at lower variable end — CAT II required; if the aircraft is only CAT I certified, it cannot legally approach with 1200 ft RVR. (4) 52 kt gust on RWY 04R with crosswind component potentially exceeding aircraft limits. (5) FZFG and BLSN: airframe icing and rapidly changing RVR. Worst-case: aircraft unable to land, diverts on minimum fuel without alternate routing plan, fuel exhaustion risk.

1-2-3 rule triggered with 1/4 SM forecast: alternate required (14 CFR §121.619).
No alternate + minimum fuel + deteriorating airport = fuel exhaustion risk on divert.
RVR 1200 ft requires CAT II-certified aircraft.
acceptable_variants: COR missed: no alternate, CAT I aircraft into CAT II conditions, fuel risk on divert.
evidence_requirements: FAA JO 7930.2: COR TAF.

prompt: A dispatcher reads METAR KBOS 231054Z 03031G43KT 1/2SM R04R/3000V4000FT SN FZFG VV008 and interprets VV008 as a cloud layer at 800 ft, concluding the ceiling is 800 ft and CAT I is authorized (DH 200 ft < ceiling). The dispatcher approves the approach. What has gone wrong and what are the consequences?
gold_decision: The error: VV008 is not a cloud layer — it is the vertical visibility group, indicating the sky is OBSCURED and the observer can see 800 ft vertically into the obscuration. This means: (1) the sky cannot be individually layered; (2) the ceiling per FAA AIM §7-1-14 is defined as the VV height when sky is obscured = 800 ft. In this case the dispatcher correctly identified 800 ft as the ceiling value, but critically misunderstood the nature of the condition — an obscured sky means the aircraft on approach cannot see any sky at any point; the entire visual envelope on short final is compromised by the obscuring medium (SN FZFG). The 800 ft figure IS the ceiling in operational terms, and CAT I is technically authorized (DH 200 ft). However, the real failure is if the dispatcher had read VV as a cloud layer and picked a different value — e.g., if no BKN/OVC layer is present, failing to use VV as the ceiling means no ceiling is identified and unrestricted airspace is assumed. Consequence of that specific misread: crew executes approach expecting VFR above 800 ft; finds total obscuration at surface; misses DH due to lost situational awareness; potential CFIT.
required_safety_constraints: VV (vertical visibility) IS the ceiling when sky is obscured — not a cloud layer (FAA AIM §7-1-14).
If VV is ignored and no BKN/OVC layer is present, the dispatcher may incorrectly conclude no ceiling exists.
Obscured sky means aircraft cannot achieve visual contact at DH; approach must be abandoned at DH if visual not acquired.
FZFG: airframe icing on approach — ice protection mandatory.
acceptable_variants: VV misread as cloud layer — if no BKN/OVC present, dispatcher may conclude no ceiling; CFIT risk on approach to obscured airport.
evidence_requirements: FAA AIM §7-1-14: VV as operational ceiling.
WMO No. 306 FM 15-XVI: VV group definition.

prompt: A dispatcher dispatches a B737 to KPHX. During the flight, the METAR updates to: KPHX 260051Z 17031G61KT 0SM +DS VV000 A2987 RMK PK WND 18061/0045. The dispatcher notes 'RVR R07L/5500VP6000FT' and tells the crew RVR is adequate. What are the consequences of this decision?
gold_decision: Fatal error: The dispatcher ignored the 0 SM prevailing visibility and VV000 (0 ft ceiling) and relied solely on the RVR reading. In an active heavy dust storm (+DS), RVR instruments measure optical properties along the runway axis — the reading may be inaccurate because the dust column is not uniform. Prevailing visibility 0 SM means the meteorological observer can see essentially nothing. VV000 = 0 ft ceiling — sky is completely obscured at the surface. Consequences: (1) Aircraft commences approach into zero visibility / zero ceiling conditions — below all published minimums for any approach category. (2) 61 kt gust: severe turbulence and structural wind loads on approach; potential loss of control. (3) Engine damage from dust ingestion — possible dual engine flame-out during approach. (4) Zero visibility: no visual contact possible at any DH; missed approach in 61 kt gusts over unfamiliar terrain. (5) Airport should have been closed — RVR is unreliable in +DS; prevailing vis and VV govern.
required_safety_constraints: VV000 = 0 ft ceiling — below ALL approach minimums; no approach authorized (FAA AIM §7-1-14).
0 SM prevailing visibility: no approach regardless of RVR (RVR unreliable in active +DS) (FAA AIM §7-1-23).
61 kt gust: extreme structural wind; engine damage risk from dust ingestion.
Dispatcher must divert immediately when 0 SM / VV000 is reported.
acceptable_variants: RVR in +DS is unreliable; VV000 and 0SM prevailing vis govern; airport must be treated as closed.
evidence_requirements: FAA AIM §7-1-14: VV000 as ceiling.
FAA AIM §7-1-23: dust storm closure.
FAA AIM §7-1-30: RVR limitations in obscuring phenomena.

prompt: A dispatcher calculates crosswind for PAOM RWY 28 using METAR PAOM 170453Z 09031G45KT using the MEAN wind (31 kt) and runway heading 280°. The dispatcher applies: angle = |280−90| = 190°, crosswind = 31 × sin(190°) = −5.4 kt (negative = treated as 5.4 kt). Approves landing. What is wrong?
gold_decision: Two errors: (1) The dispatcher used the mean wind speed (31 kt) instead of the gust (45 kt) for crosswind evaluation. FAA-H-8083-25C Ch. 5 requires the gust speed for limit evaluation. (2) The angle calculation is incorrect. Wind 090° on runway 280°: difference = |280−90| = 190°. When the angle exceeds 180°, use the supplement: 360−190 = 170°. Then sin(170°) = sin(10°) = 0.174. Crosswind = 45 × 0.174 ≈ 7.8 kt. But the dispatcher also overlooked that cos(170°) = cos(10°) = −0.985, which is negative, meaning this is a TAILWIND component: 45 × (−0.985) ≈ −44 kt → 44 kt tailwind on RWY 28. The wind is almost directly behind the aircraft on RWY 28. Landing with a 44 kt tailwind extends landing distance dramatically and likely exceeds performance limits for a B737. The correct action is to use RWY 10 (wind nearly on the nose).
required_safety_constraints: Use gust speed for crosswind evaluation (FAA-H-8083-25C Ch. 5).
Angles >180° require supplement calculation: 360° minus angle for sin/cos.
44 kt tailwind on RWY 28: landing distance massively increased; likely exceeds performance limit.
RWY 10 is the correct runway — wind 090° gives near-headwind on RWY 10 (heading 100°).
acceptable_variants: Mean wind used instead of gust; angle calc error; 44 kt tailwind on RWY 28; use RWY 10.
evidence_requirements: FAA-H-8083-25C Chapter 5: crosswind and headwind components.
FAA AIM §7-1-30: wind group in METAR.

prompt: A dispatcher reviews TAF KFAR 290520Z and sees TEMPO 2906/2909 3/4SM BLSN. The dispatcher decides: 'TEMPO only lasts <1 hour at a time — the 3/4 SM BLSN is just temporary. The base forecast (2 SM) is fine for the 1-2-3 rule.' Consequently no alternate is filed. ETA is 0700Z. What consequences result?
gold_decision: The dispatcher misapplied the TEMPO rule. The 1-2-3 rule (14 CFR §121.619) requires an alternate if, during the ETA ±1 hr window (0600–0800Z), the forecast conditions are below 2000 ft ceiling OR 3 SM visibility. The BASE forecast shows BKN009 (900 ft) < 2000 ft and 2 SM < 3 SM — BOTH thresholds trigger the alternate requirement from the BASE forecast alone, even without considering the TEMPO. The dispatcher's error was focusing on the TEMPO as 'temporary' while ignoring that the BASE forecast itself triggers the 1-2-3 rule. Additionally, TEMPO only modifies conditions temporarily but does not eliminate the base forecast — during the remaining portion of the TEMPO window, the base 2 SM BKN009 conditions still apply and they themselves are below both thresholds. Consequence: no alternate filed; aircraft arrives in 3/4 SM BLSN (or 2 SM BKN009 base conditions); cannot land; diverts on minimum fuel with no alternate routing.
required_safety_constraints: 1-2-3 rule applies to the BASE forecast first — base BKN009 = 900 ft < 2000 ft triggers alternate regardless of TEMPO (14 CFR §121.619).
TEMPO 'temporary' exception does NOT exempt the base forecast from the 1-2-3 check.
Base forecast 2 SM < 3 SM: second trigger on visibility.
Alternate required even if TEMPO were absent.
acceptable_variants: Base forecast BKN009/2SM triggers 1-2-3 rule independent of TEMPO; alternate required.
evidence_requirements: FAA AIM §7-1-28: TEMPO does not replace base forecast.

However, the systemic failure of not recognizing VV as a ceiling is dangerous: in other scenarios where VV is close to DH, this error would authorize approaches below minimums.

prompt: A dispatcher reading TAF KBOS 230537Z: 'FM230800 05026G43KT 1/4SM +SN OVC015 WS020/05050KT' for a flight with ETA 0900Z files an alternate. gold_decision: The dispatcher correctly filed an alternate but gave incorrect advice about the WS group. WS020/05050KT is a low-level wind shear (LLWS) advisory in the TAF — it indicates non-convective wind shear at 2000 ft AGL with winds from 050° at 50 kt, contrasting sharply with surface wind 050/26G43KT. This is not a 'wind aloft advisory' — it is a forecast of significant shear below 2000 ft, directly affecting the approach and initial missed approach phase. The crew not being briefed on LLWS means: (1) they will not apply LLWS penetration techniques (VREF +20 kt) on approach; (2) if shear is encountered at 2000 ft AGL, the aircraft may lose 30+ kt of airspeed suddenly; (3) speed decay on approach can cause undershoot below approach path; (4) at low altitude (below 2000 ft), recovery from severe LLWS may not be possible before ground contact.

LLWS at 2000 ft: crew must apply VREF +20 kt or carrier-specific wind shear additive.
'Wind aloft advisory' characterization is incorrect — LLWS is a below-2000 ft approach hazard.
Dispatcher must transmit LLWS briefing to crew via ACARS or pre-departure briefing.
acceptable_variants: WS020/05050KT is LLWS advisory; crew must be briefed; 50 kt at 2000 ft creates severe shear on approach.

prompt: An agent analyzing dispatch to KFAR with TAF showing 'TEMPO 2906/2909 3/4SM BLSN' for ETA 0800Z concludes: 'TEMPO ends at 0900Z — ETA 0800Z is within the TEMPO window. However, TEMPO means each occurrence lasts <1 hour and total <50% of the period. The TEMPO is 3 hours long, so statistically only about 1.5 hours will be affected. The 0800Z ETA is likely to fall in a non-TEMPO period.' What is the error?
gold_decision: The agent's probabilistic reasoning misapplies the TEMPO definition. TEMPO specifying <50% of the period means the conditions will NOT be continuous, but it provides NO guarantee that any specific time within the window (like 0800Z ETA) is in a non-TEMPO period. From a dispatch planning perspective, TEMPO conditions must be treated as POSSIBLE at any time during the stated window. The dispatcher cannot estimate that 'ETA falls in a non-TEMPO period' based on the 50% rule. The correct approach: if the TEMPO window overlaps ETA±1 hr and the TEMPO conditions (3/4 SM BLSN) are below 1-2-3 thresholds (< 3 SM), the alternate requirement applies. The base forecast (BKN009/2SM) already triggers the rule independently. The agent's probabilistic inference is not an authorized method under 14 CFR §121.619.
required_safety_constraints: TEMPO timing: <50% of period is a statistical definition, NOT a guarantee for any specific ETA time (ICAO Annex 3).
Cannot apply probability inference to exempt ETA from TEMPO consideration.
1-2-3 rule uses worst-case TEMPO conditions during the ETA window, not mean conditions.
Base forecast BKN009/2SM independently triggers alternate requirement.
acceptable_variants: TEMPO probability inference is not authorized for 1-2-3 rule; worst-case TEMPO conditions apply; alternate required.
evidence_requirements: ICAO Annex 3 §6.4: TEMPO definition.
14 CFR §121.619: 1-2-3 alternate rule — no probability exception.

prompt: An agent processes the METAR PAOM 170453Z 09031G45KT 1/4SM R28/1400V2200FT SN FZFG VV005 M14/M16 and concludes that the approach is authorized for CAT I aircraft because 'R28 upper RVR 2200 ft exceeds CAT I minimum.' gold_decision: Compound failures: (1) RVR: variable lower end 1400 ft < CAT I minimum 1800 ft — CAT I NOT authorized during 1400 ft periods. The upper end (2200 ft) is irrelevant; the lower end governs. (2) Wind 090°/31G45KT on RWY 28 (heading 280°): angle = 190° → supplement 170°; cos(170°) = cos(10°) = 0.985 but the direction is from the tail: headwind/tailwind = 45 × cos(170°) = 45 × (−0.985) ≈ −44 kt → 44 kt TAILWIND. A 44 kt tailwind on landing is catastrophic: landing distance increases by a factor of 2–3; the aircraft will overrun the runway at any reasonable landing speed; no commercial aircraft has a 44 kt tailwind limit (most are ≤10 kt). Using RWY 28 with a 44 kt tailwind could result in runway excursion. The correct action is to use RWY 10 (reciprocal; headwind).
required_safety_constraints: Lower RVR end (1400 ft) is controlling — CAT I not authorized (FAA AIM §7-1-30).
Tailwind 44 kt on RWY 28 — use RWY 10; 44 kt tailwind far exceeds any commercial aircraft tailwind limit.
Failure to compute tailwind component is a systematic omission in crosswind/headwind analysis (FAA-H-8083-25C Ch. 5).
acceptable_variants: Lower RVR controls; 44 kt tailwind exceeds all limits; RWY 10 must be used.
evidence_requirements: FAA AIM §7-1-30.

prompt: An agent reads METAR KBOS 231254Z 02033G45KT 1/4SM R04R/1400V1800FT +SN FZFG VV005 and states: 'PK WND 03053/1227 = 53 kt peak gust. This is just a historical remark — the current wind group (33G45KT) is what I should use. Approach authorized for CAT II on 1400 ft lower RVR.' What is wrong?
gold_decision: The agent's conclusion about PK WND is partially correct (current wind governs for approach planning) but incomplete in its safety assessment. PK WND 03053KT at 1227Z means a 53 kt gust occurred 27 minutes ago — conditions are this severe and may recur. More critically, the agent's CAT II authorization is flawed: R04R lower RVR 1400 ft > CAT II minimum 1200 ft — CAT II is authorized on RVR. However, wind 020°/33G45KT on RWY 04R (heading 040°): angle = |40−20| = 20°; crosswind = 45 × sin(20°) ≈ 15 kt — within limits. Headwind = 45 × cos(20°) = 42 kt.
